description: Documents describe governance accountability and responsibilities There is an organizational chart or document, which describes the lines of authority and accountability from governance and within the service. 1.1.13 The responsibilities of governance include providing support to the personnel in the health facility. The support from organization managers includes regular supervisory visits, policies, guidelines, monitoring, written communication and education
The mortuary managers ensure that policies and procedures guide and support the activities and management of the mortuaries
A designated personnel member is responsible for compiling and indexing policies and procedures, and ensuring their circulation, recall and review
Policies and procedures are signed/endorsed by persons authorized to do so
Policies and procedures are compiled into a comprehensive manual, which is indexed and easily accessible to all personnel members
All policies and procedures are reviewed at appropriate intervals, dated and signed
There is a mechanism for ensuring that policies are known and implemented
There are documented processes for staffing the mortuary company’s mortuaries. The desired education, qualifications, skills and knowledge are defined for all personnel members
Documented personnel information is maintained on each personnel member
Each personnel member's responsibilities are defined in a current job description, which is agreed to by the employee
All personnel members are orientated and inducted to the mortuary and to their specific job responsibilities at the time of appointment
Each personnel member receives ongoing in - service education and training to maintain or advance his/her skills and knowledge, based on identified needs
There is a process in place to recruit personnel
There is a process in place to evaluate the qualifications of new personnel
There is a process in place to appoint, evaluate and terminate personnel. Personnel members employed in the service have a written job description, which defines their responsibilities
There is at least one documented evaluation of personnel each year, or more frequently, as defined by the organisation
There are mutually agreed policies and procedures with the personnel for the satisfactory conduct of industrial relations activities
Written disciplinary procedures, which meet the requirements of current legislation, are available
There is a grievance procedure in terms of current legislation
There are dispute and appeal procedures
There are recognition agreements for trade unions and/or health professional organizations
The mortuary manager/supervisor is responsible and accountable for the day- to - d a y operation of the mortuary service
The manager has the education and experience to carry out his or her responsibilities
The manager ensures that approved policies are carried out
The manager ensures compliance with applicable Laws and regulations. 3.4.14
The manager implements processes to manage and control human, financial and other resources

The manager is involved in the preparation financial estimates for the mortuary. There are designated individuals appointed to act in the absence of the mortuary manager/supervisor to provide the service with direction at all times
The manager/supervisor ensures that policies and procedures are available to guide the service
The manager/supervisor provides guidelines on professional conduct within the mortuary service
Policies and procedures are signed by persons authorized to do so Policies and procedures are compiled in a comprehensive manual, which is indexed and easily accessible to all personnel
Each policy and procedure is reviewed, dated and signed
There is a mechanism to ensure that policies and procedures are known to and implemented by personnel working in the mortuary service

There is a written, planned orientation and induction programme for mortuary service personnel who are new to the service. The orientation and induction programme prepares personnel for their roles and responsibilities in the mortuary service
The orientation and induction programme includes instruction on the manner of communication with visitors to the mortuary
The orientation and induction programme includes health and safety and infection control policies as they apply to the mortuary service

The mortuary service provides documented in - service training programmes for its personnel consistent with service objectives. The in -service training programme ensures that all personnel are competent and updated when new systems and equipment are installed
The in-service training programme provides supervisory training
Personnel in the service receive in -service training to ensure competency
The in-service training programme includes on -going training in emergency procedures (e.g., fire drill and security)
Records of the holding of in -service training sessions are kept, including records of attendance

There is evidence that permits required by the local authority for the carrying out of mortuary operation are current. Facilities and equipment are maintained to provide clean and safe working conditions which comply with relevant legislation
Mortuary vehicles comply with specifications applicable to the transport of human bodies
All areas are provided with adequate lighting and ventilation
Washing facilities (showers and hand-wash basin with hot and cold water are provided

Lockers for storing personal clothing and property are provided away from work areas and are not used to store work clothing and personal protective equipment. Personnel who use equipment are trained on its use
Records of equipment maintenance are available
Records of equipment maintenance are available
Records of stocks of chemicals, cleaning agents and detergents are maintained
Hazardous and flammable materials are stored in accordance with relevant regulations
Arrangements, including lockable doors are used to provide security for the mortuary
There is adequate storage space to enable rapid retrieval and removal of equipment when needed

Policies and procedures guiding the storage of bodies in the mortuary are available. Bodies known or suspected of harbouring infectious diseases are contained within a body bag of approved specifications
Bodies are stored at a prescribed temperature
The temperature of each refrigerator is checked and recorded twice daily
Emergency power is available for the refrigerators
Access to the refrigerators is controlled
Manual lifting of bodies is minimized by the provision of hoists and/or elevating trolleys. There is documented evidence of approval for the removal of bodies
A register is kept for the storage and removal of bodies
The autopsy table and dissection bench is provided with shadow- free lighting
Adequate ventilation is provided for dissection benches where formalin fixed organs and tissues are handled
The floor surface is impervious, easy to clean, sealed at the edges and provided with drains, which have filtered traps
There is adequate storage for equipment and instruments
Working bays are large enough to enable personnel to work without being crowded
Instruments and other equipment required for an autopsy is readily accessible to each working bay
The mortuary theatre has facilities for weighing and measuring organs
The mortuary theatre has facilities for photographic evidence collection
There is a designated viewing area separated from the body storage section
The waiting room of the viewing area is suitably furnished
There is sufficient space for seating and a mortuary trolley in the viewing area
Toilet facilities are available within easy access of the viewing area
The mortuary manager/supervisor ensures that the mortuary personnel demonstrate respect for the deceased
The personnel are courteous and display respect towards the deceased and their families at all times
There is a documented risk management plan for the mortuary
Risk assessments are recorded and the results are made available to personnel
There is a mechanism to ensure that personnel are aware of risks and their consequences
The mortuary implements response plan to likely community emergencies, epidemics and natural or other disasters
An internal incident and emergency plan is in place
A monitoring programme provides data on incidents, injuries and other events that support planning and further risk reduction
Policies and procedures are available relating to the handling and storage of bodies
Policies and procedures are available relating to the isolation of bodies suspected of harbouring infectious diseases
Policies and procedures are available relating to the wearing of protective clothing
Policies and procedures are available relating to the colour coding of bags for soiled and infected linen and their handling, processing and disposal
There is a process to deal with used, discarded or contaminated protective clothing
First aid is available to provide immediate attention to all injuries
An emergency shower or eye-wash facility is provided in the washroom area and where chemicals are stored or handled
Counseling and treatment is available to personnel who have suffered a sharps injury
Processes for regular debriefing sessions for employees are in place
The infection control processes include prevention of the spread of blood -borne diseases (such as HIV, and hepatitis B/C) and focuses on processes that may lead to infection
The processes are compliant with National laws and regulations
All personnel, vehicles, and other areas of the mortuary are included in the infection control processes
The mortuary has established the processes associated with infection risk and developed procedures to reduce infection risk
The mortuary identifies those situations for which protective clothing is required
The mortuary identifies those areas where hand washing and disinfecting procedures are required
Hand washing and disinfecting procedures are used correctly in those areas
Gloves, masks, soap and disinfectants are available and are used correctly when required
Secure storage areas and well-maintained equipment are available to housekeeping personnel
Mops and brooms are cleaned and dried before being stored
Cleaning cupboards are adequately ventilated
Access to entrances, exits and emergency equipment is kept free of obstruction
Soiled linen is placed in bags designated for that purpose
Linen is not stacked close to heating or cooling equipment, the vents of motors or air -conditioning units
Soiled linen is stored in a secure facility
A separate storage area is set aside for combustible refuse, waste paper and empty containers pending their removal from the premises
Pest control mechanisms are identified and implemented
There is a plan for the inventory, handling, storage and use of clinical materials and the control and disposal of materials and waste
(b) Health wastes are managed according to a plan
The plan includes labeling of hazardous materials and wastes
The plan identifies documentation requirements including any permits, licenses, or other regulatory requirements
The plan includes the procedures to be adopted when a spill of a hazardous chemical or infectious material occurs
The plan includes the proper disposal of health and other waste
Fire fighting equipment is regularly inspected and serviced at least annually and the date of the service is recorded on the apparatus
A 24-hour security system is maintained for the routine monitoring and safeguarding of the building, personnel and visitors
The powers and duties of security officers are clearly defined
Safety and security systems are developed with input from personnel
Security systems include access control of defined protected areas
The building is evaluated to identify areas requiring security doors and locks
For each component of the mortuaries risk management programme, there is planned education to ensure that personnel can effectively carry out their responsibilities
Personnel can describe and/or demonstrate their role in response to a fire
Personnel can describe and/or demonstrate actions to eliminate, minimize, or report safety, security and other risks
Personnel can describe and/or demonstrate precautions, procedures and participation in the storage, handling, segregation, disposal of hazardous wastes and materials, in related emergencies
Personnel can describe and/or demonstrate procedures and their role in internal and community emergencies and disasters
Personnel knowledge is tested regarding their role in maintaining a safe and effective facility
Personnel training and testing is documented as to who was trained and tested, and the results
The organisation has a plan to meet information needs, based on an assessment of the needs of those within and outside the organisation
The information needs of those who provide pathology services are considered in the planning process
The information needs and requirements of individuals and agencies outside the organisation are considered in the planning process
The information plan includes how the confidentiality, security and integrity of data and information will be maintained
Security and confidentiality of deceased person data and information are maintained
The mortuary has a process to aggregate data and has determined what data and information are to be regularly aggregated to meet the needs of the organisation
Aggregate data and information to support organisation management
Aggregate data and information to support the quality management programme
The specific content of records has been determined by the organisation
Those prescribed to make entries in the record are identified in organisation policy
The format and location of entries are determined by organisation policy
There is a process to ensure that only authorised individuals make entries in records
The organisation has a policy on the retention of records and other data and information
The retention process provides expected confidentiality and security
Records, data and information are retained according to policy or laws and regulations
Records, data and information are destroyed appropriately
There are formalised quality improvement processes for the service that have been developed and agreed upon by the personnel of the service
There is a training programme for Personnel that is consistent with their role in the quality management and improvement programme
Indicators of performance are identified to evaluate the quality of service rendered
The quality improvement cycle includes the monitoring and evaluation of the standards set and the remedial action implemented
A documentation audit system is in place
Information on the quality management and improvement programme is communicated to personnel regularly
The organisation documents the improvements achieved and sustained
This information leads to the development of processes to ensure that quality is sustained. Criteria
There is a trained and licensed Pathologist who is accountable for the oversight of all autopsy services of the organisation
The pathologist is accountable for the development, implementation, and monitoring of autopsy service functions
The pathologist is accountable for the in -service training/education and performance monitoring of employees that provide autopsy services
A determination is made about the current qualifications of individuals to provide pathology services
The organisation has an effective process to authorise all health professionals to provide pathology services consistent with their qualifications
The head of department/medical director is accountable for the medical components of the quality management and improvement programme
The medical director and the managerial leaders select pathology practice guidelines
The medical director and the managerial leaders adapt guidelines as appropriate for the clients served by the organisation and the resources available within the organisation
Guidelines are reviewed and adapted on a regular basis after implementation
The forensic pathology service removes the body from the scene of death in consultation with the Investigation Officer after death certification by a qualified medical practitioner
The body is handled according to prescribed policies and procedures
The body is transported to the mortuary according to stipulated procedures after it has been certified dead by the hospital Accident and Emergency Department or Clinic doctors
Designated vehicles are used for the transportation of bo dies
The medico -legal admission requirements and processes are adhered to when a body is admitted
The body is stored in accordance with the prescribed procedure
Access to the body is controlled at all times
Medico-legal post mortems are conducted within prescribed time frames. For forensic post -mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist secures custodianship of the body from the scene of death

For forensic post-mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist will have the full authority to discretionally allow access to the body. Access to the body will be limited to those who have a medical background and fulfil the criteria
For forensic post -mortem examinations, the body shall be viewed by the family representative for purposes of identification only
For forensic post-mortem examinations, the family may elect to have a medical practitioner observe the Post -mortem procedure
For forensic post -mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist maintains the chain of evidence relating to the body and any associated items
For forensic post -mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist conducts the post mortem investigation and harvesting of evidentiary material according to prescribed laws and procedures
For forensic post -mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist conducts appropriate special investigations according to prescribed laws and procedures
For forensic post -mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist produces medico -legal reports, consultancy reports, expert testimony and opinions
For forensic post -mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist archives documents, specimens and related materials
For forensic post -mortem examinations, the forensic pathologist releases the body to a registered undertaker or for a pauper’s burial in terms of relevant legislation
For clinical post -mortem examinations, the anatomical pathologist conducts the post mortem examination and harvesting of diagnostic material
For clinical post -mortem examinations consent from relatives should be obtained before the case is admitted for post -mortem examination
For clinical post -mortem examinations, the anatomical pathologist conducts appropriate special investigations
For clinical post -mortem examinations the anatomical pathologist conducts the post mortem examination and harvesting of diagnostic material
For clinical post -mortem examinations, the anatomical pathologist produces reports and submits it to the requesting entity
For clinical post -mortem examinations, the anatomical pathologist archives documents, specimens and related materials according to national regulations
Following anatomical post -mortem examinations, the mortuary personnel release the body to a registered undertaker or for a pauper’s burial in terms of relevant legislation
